title: Zheng He
text:
Doctoral Candidate (Dr. med.)
🏛 Starkuvienė Lab, BioQuant, Heidelberg University · Heidelberg 🏥 Medizinische Fakultät Mannheim, Heidelberg University · Mannheim
Supervised by Prof. Dr. med. Michael Keese & Prof. Dr. Vytautė Starkuvienė-Erfle Doktorvater: PD Dr. med. Martin Sigl

✉ zheng.he@bioquant.uni-heidelberg.de ✉ hezheng.surgeon@gmail.com 🎓 Google Scholar ORCID GitHub
My doctoral research spans three interconnected lines of investigation, combining computational transcriptomics, quantitative imaging, and experimental cell biology.
VSMC phenotypic plasticity — My primary project focuses on defining the transcriptional basis of vascular smooth muscle cell (VSMC) phenotypic switching — the transition between a quiescent contractile state and a proliferative synthetic state central to atherosclerosis and in-stent restenosis. Using a donor-aware statistical framework on large-scale human aortic SMC transcriptomic data, I identified a 1,103-gene switching program capturing a proliferation-versus-cholesterol-metabolism axis, with demonstrated transferability across growth-factor- and cytokine-driven perturbation contexts and anchoring to human atherosclerotic plaque. Experimental validation employs a PDGF-BB induction model in primary human aortic SMCs, using resveratrol as a pharmacological counter-stimulus to test the functional and pharmacological responsiveness of the computationally defined program.
ABCB5⁺ MSCs & chronic wound healing — A second line of research concerns ABCB5⁺ MSCs — a rare population of skin-derived stromal cells with potent immunomodulatory and regenerative properties. Beyond a published review (Journal of Clinical Medicine, 2025), I am leading a multi-modal bioinformatics study integrating an expression-derived paracrine candidate atlas, chronic wound transcriptome profiling, scRNA-seq contextualization, cell-cell communication analysis, immune infiltration profiling, Mendelian randomization, and molecular docking — converging on ECM remodeling, complement-mediated immunoregulation, and CXCL12-driven chemotactic crosstalk as core repair axes. Computational findings are complemented by experimental co-culture studies examining the direct paracrine effects of ABCB5⁺ MSCs on wound-relevant cell populations.
Endocytic trafficking in HeLa cells — Working within the Starkuvienė Lab, I investigate endocytic pathway regulation in HeLa Kyoto cells using size-selective dextran-FITC uptake assays (10/40/70 kDa), pathway-specific pharmacological inhibition (Wortmannin for PI3K-dependent macropinocytosis; Dynasore for dynamin-dependent endocytosis), 3D spheroid culture models, and wound healing migration assays. This work contributes to the lab’s programme on spatial and temporal regulation of intracellular trafficking and its dysregulation in disease.

title: About
text:
I am a medical doctor and doctoral candidate (Dr. med.) affiliated with two institutions at Heidelberg University. My primary research is conducted at BioQuant, Heidelberg University under the supervision of Prof. Dr. Vytautė Starkuvienė-Erfle, while my doctoral degree is registered at the Medizinische Fakultät Mannheim under Prof. Dr. med. Michael Keese, who oversees my research project, funding, and thesis. The formal Doktorvater is PD Dr. med. Martin Sigl.
My work bridges clinical vascular medicine and computational biology, focusing on the molecular mechanisms underlying VSMC phenotypic plasticity.
Before my doctoral studies, I worked as a cardiovascular surgeon at the Third Affiliated Hospital of Chongqing Medical University (2020–2021), where I gained clinical experience in vascular and endovascular procedures.

title: Research
text:
Vascular smooth muscle cells (VSMCs) are highly plastic cells capable of transitioning between a quiescent contractile phenotype and a proliferative synthetic phenotype — a switch central to the pathogenesis of atherosclerosis and in-stent restenosis.
My primary doctoral project defines the transcriptional basis of this switch through a multi-stage computational and experimental framework:

Discovery — Using a donor-aware limma model on GSE193817 (284 samples, 148 donors, 136 paired), I identified a 1,103-gene switching program (884 UP, 219 DN; FDR < 0.05) capturing a proliferation-versus-cholesterol-metabolism transcriptional axis.
Transferability validation — The program demonstrates context-selective transferability across multiple independent perturbation datasets (growth-factor/cytokine contexts: 5/5 concordant; metabolic/stress contexts: 0/4), validated in GSE69637 and GSE253003.
Human plaque anchoring — The program is anchored to human atherosclerotic plaque states using single-cell data (GSE155512), with strong canonical SMC enrichment confirmed.
Experimental validation — A PDGF-BB induction model in primary human aortic SMCs (HAoSMC) uses resveratrol as a pharmacological counter-stimulus to test whether the computationally defined program is functionally and pharmacologically responsive (partial resveratrol rescue endpoint).

Target journal: Arteriosclerosis, Thrombosis, and Vascular Biology (ATVB)
Key tools: R (limma, WGCNA, clusterProfiler, GSVA), Western Blot, Immunofluorescence, RT-qPCR

title: Research
text:
ABCB5⁺ MSCs & Chronic Wound Healing
ABCB5⁺ MSCs are a rare population of skin-derived stromal cells defined by expression of the ATP-binding cassette transporter ABCB5, with potent immunomodulatory and tissue-regenerative properties.
This line of research has two components:
Published review — A comprehensive review of the differentiation and regeneration potential of ABCB5⁺ MSCs and their clinical translation perspectives, published in Journal of Clinical Medicine (2025).
Original bioinformatics study — A systems-level, multi-modal analysis of the paracrine role of ABCB5⁺ MSCs in chronic wound repair, structured around an expression-derived paracrine candidate atlas integrated with:

Chronic wound transcriptome profiling (WGCNA co-expression analysis)
Convergent target prioritization (M1 atlas ∩ wound DEG modules → 10 hub genes)
Single-cell RNA-seq contextualization + cell-cell communication analysis (CellChat)
Immune infiltration profiling (ssGSEA)
Mendelian randomization boundary testing
Molecular docking for structural plausibility

The analysis converges on three tissue-local repair axes: ECM remodeling (DCN, FBLN1, FBLN2, MFAP4, SERPINF1), complement-mediated immunoregulation (C3, CFH, SERPING1), and CXCL12-driven chemotactic crosstalk. The manuscript is under final preparation for submission, with parallel wet-lab co-culture validation underway.

title: Research
text:
Endocytic Trafficking in HeLa Cells
Working within the Starkuvienė Lab at BioQuant, I investigate endocytic pathway regulation in HeLa Kyoto cells using:

Size-selective dextran-FITC uptake assays (10/40/70 kDa) — fluid-phase endocytosis quantification across molecular weight ranges
Pathway-specific pharmacological inhibition — Wortmannin (PI3K-dependent macropinocytosis) and Dynasore (dynamin-dependent endocytosis) to dissect pathway contributions
3D spheroid culture models — Nunclon Sphera ultra-low attachment platform for validating endocytic behaviour in structurally relevant 3D context
Wound healing migration assays — scratch assay with quantitative imaging analysis

This work contributes to the lab’s broader programme on how spatial and temporal regulation of intracellular trafficking is achieved and how its dysregulation contributes to disease and aging.

title: Research
text:
Methods Overview







Category
Techniques




Bioinformatics
Bulk RNA-seq (limma, edgeR), scRNA-seq (Seurat), WGCNA, GSVA, GSEA, Mendelian Randomization (TwoSampleMR, coloc), CellChat, ssGSEA, molecular docking (HDOCK)


Wet Lab
Western Blot, Immunofluorescence, RT-qPCR, Wound Healing Assay, Cell Viability (CTB), Dextran Uptake Assay, 3D Spheroid Culture


Cell Models
Primary human aortic SMCs (HAoSMC), HeLa cells, ABCB5⁺ MSCs


Languages
R, Python
